pattern: The cheapest way to make one fixed-size window constant is to level it to its own median, so the whole problem reduces to picking k non-overlapping windows with the smallest total median-leveling cost -- a sliding-median sweep feeds a small interval scheduling dynamic program.
hints: For a single window of size x, the fewest operations to make every element equal is achieved by leveling them all to the window's median -- the sum of absolute differences to the median is the window's cost.

Slide a window of size x across the whole array, maintaining its median and total absolute deviation incrementally (a two-heap running median) so every window's cost is known in roughly O(n log x) total instead of resorting each time.

With every window's cost precomputed, choosing k non-overlapping windows to minimize total cost is a small interval-scheduling dynamic program: dp[i][j] is the best cost using the first i elements with j windows placed so far, transitioning either by skipping position i or by closing a window of length x ending there.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, two windows of size three
statement example, two windows of size two
the array is already made of equal adjacent pairs, so zero operations are needed
the tightest fit possible, one window covering the whole array
negative and positive values already forming equal pairs, testing that leveling cost handles negatives correctly
an alternating pattern where every window has the same nonzero cost, so the DP must still pick non-overlapping ones correctly
only one window needed, chosen from several overlapping candidates

stress.description: random arrays near the maximum length with a small window and window count
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(100000, size))
    nums = [rng.randint(-1000000, 1000000) for _ in range(n)]
    x = rng.randint(2, min(50, n))
    max_k = min(15, n // x)
    k = rng.randint(1, max(1, max_k))
    return [nums, x, k]
